content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
March 12, 1990
Lance H. olson
O1son, Conne11y, Hagel, Fong and Leidigh
300 capitol Mal1, Suite 35o
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re: Your Request for Advice
our rile No. A-90-118
Dear Mr. Olson:
In your letter of February 5, ]-990, you requested our advice
concerning the reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the trActtr).1 Your request j.s on behalf of Assenbl)rmember Ton
Hayden, a candidate for Assembly in 1990 and a proPonent of the
EnvironDental Protection Act of 1990 initiative 
measure.
OUESTTON
Assembllmember Hayden is on the steering committee of a
conmittee formed to support qualification and passage of the
Environmental Protection Act of 1990 initiative 
measure. other
candidates who are proponents of that sarue initiative 
neasure are
considering forning their own comrnittees to support its passage
and qualification.
will Assenblymember Eayden or the other candidates/proponents
have rnade or received an in-kind contribution from other
candidates as a result of the consultation and coordination
between the various candi,date-controlled ba11ot measure committees
formed to support the initiative 
roeasure?
CONCLUSION
Under the facts given, Assenbl]rnember Hayden and the other
candidates/proponents will not have nade or received an in-kind
1 co.r"rrr."nt code sections 810oo-91015. A1I statutory references
are to the covernment code unless otherwise indicated. Connission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. AII references to regulations are to Title 2,
oivision 6 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4.O8O7 o (916)322-5660


File No. A-9o-118
Page 2
contribution from the other candidate-controlled ballot measure
cornrnittees as a result of consultation and coordination betlreen
the various conmittees formed to support qualification and passage
of the Environmental Protection Act of 1990 initiative 
measure.
In light of the answer to this question, it is unnecessary
for us to address other questions raised in your letter concerning
the propriety and reporting of in-kind contributions.
FACTS
Assembl)rnember Hayden is an incurnbent representi.ng the 44th
Assenbl-y District. 
He expects to be a candidate for re-election
on the 1990 ballot. 
He is also a proponent (as that term is used
in Elections code Section 3502) of the Environmental Protection
Act of 1990 initiative 
rneasure ('rEnvironmental Initiative
Measurerr ) .
The Hayden comrittee is a recipient connittee organized
pursuant to Section 82013 (c) . The purpose of the Hayden connittee
is to support the qualification and passage of the Environmental
Initiative 
l,leasure.
The Hayden connittee is governed by a steering comruittee
involving representatives frorn several environmental organizations
and Assembl]rnenber Hayden. The Hayden connittee has registered as
a candidate-controlled comroittee pursuant to section 82015.
Assembl]rnember Hayden is one of seven proponents of the
Environmental Initiative lteasure. of the renaining six
proponents,' ti{o others are also elected public officials. 
These
officials 
have forned or are considering forruing their own
individually controlled baI1ot measure commj,ttees for the purpose
of gualifying and prornoting the passage of the Environrnental
Initiative 
l.teasure.
Assemblymember Hayden has transferred funds from hi,s re-
election cornmittee to the Hayden connittee for purposes of paying
the various expenses of the Connittee. The other officials 
have
or will transfer funds fron their election committee to their own
individually controlled baIIot neasure cornmittees.
During the course of the qualification process the various
proponents, including the elected officials 
and their respective
controlled baLlot neasure connittees, expect to consult with one
another and coordinate efforts. 
Assuning the Environmental
Initiative 
lleasure qualifies, sinilar consultation and
coordination is likeIy to occur during the canpaign phase of the
election.
Although the candidates and their controlled bal1ot measure
cornroittees will consult rrith each other, each conmi.ttee wiII pay
its own operating and canpaign expenses. For example, each
coDaittee $ril1 pay for its own petitions, canpaign consultants,


File No. A-90-118
Page 3
fundraising expenses, direct mail, rnedia buys, promotional
materials, etc. 
However, the committees nay well use the same
consultants, direct mail vendors, and pollsters. 
when vendors are
shared, costs wiLl be apportioned and paid by each committee.
ANALYSTS
In Advice Letter, No. A-82-095 (copy enclosed), we explained
that palment by a controlled committee for one-half of the cost of
a ballot measure comnittee's advertisement mentioning the
candidate in a way which woutd otherwj,se give rise to an in-kind
contribution would be permissible under the Act.
we also note that the Hiltachk Advice Letter, No. A-89-533
(copy enclosed), confirmed telephone advice that transfers of
contributions from a candidate-controlled cornmittee to a ballot
measure committee controlled by another candidate are prohibited
by the Act. The Hiltachk letter states that a candidate-
cintrolled commitE--z may rnake contributj.ons to a ballot measure
commj,ttee3 from carnpaign funds but that this rule does not appl,y
$rhere the committee uraking the contribution is controlled by one
candidate, and the conmittee receiving the contrlbution is
controlled by another candidate.4
A ttcontribution" is a paynent for political 
purposes, r,vhere
the recipient does not provide adequate consideration to the
contributor. 
(Section 82015.) If the costs of the goods and
services supplied by each vendor are apportioned equally and the
palments of the apportioned costs are made directly by each
candidate's committee and the ballot neasure cornmittee, this would
no! constitsute a contribution by or to any of the candidate
2 Urrd"a section 820: 6 t a controLfed comnittee is rra committee
which is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate or state
measure proponent or which acts jointly with a candidate,
controlted committee or state measure proponent in connection with
the rnaking of expenditures. A candidate or state neasure
proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or any other
cornrnittee he controls has a significant influence on the actions
or decisi.ons of the committee."
3 Section 82043 defines a neasure as rrany constitutional
amendment or other proposition which is subnitted to a popular
vote at an election by action of a legislative body, or which is
subnitted or is intended to be subnitted to a popular vote at an
electj,on by initiative, 
referenduD or recall procedure whether or
not it qualifies for the bal-lot.rl
4 Th" advice in the Hiltachk tetter nay be reconsidered by the
Conmission, depending upon the Conmission,s decision in a pending
opinion request, fn re l.tccarthv, No. 89-007. The Mccarthv opinion
also concerns the activities of candidate-control led ballot
neasure commi-ttees.


Fi■e No. A-90-■■8
Page 4
controlled baIlot measure committees. Since that is the situati.on
you describe in your letter, there is no contribution under the
facts stated.
This advice is lilrited to interpretation of the Political
Reforn Act.
If you have any questions concerning this letter, please
contact ne at (915) 322-5901.
sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
Legal Division
